file.content:
# Candidate Instructions

## What This Assignment Tests

This isn't about whether you can build a CRUD app. It's about:

- **Domain modeling** - Can you research a problem domain and decompose it into the right abstractions?

  Onboarding quickly into complex client domains is part of this job — that's why we chose the insurance domain. Treat domain research as part of the work, not a preliminary step. Research claims processing, coverage rules, denial taxonomies, and adjudication logic before and alongside building.

  You don't need to be an insurance expert, but you should be able to speak in the language you've modeled in your code and explain *why* you modeled a business rule a certain way. This is what lets you think through business rule changes without depending entirely on AI — otherwise you can't provide meaningful feedback or take ownership of the final code.
- **Engineering judgment** - Can you make good decisions about what to build and what to skip?
- **Test-driven thinking** - Do you write tests that encode your understanding of the domain, not just verify UI behavior?
- **AI collaboration** - Can you work effectively with AI tools while staying in control?
- **Self-awareness** - Do you know what's good and what's rough in your own work?

---

## Ground Rules

- **Ship a working application, not just a passing test suite.** We will clone your submission, set it up from your README, and run the flows you built. If it doesn't run, the tests don't count.
- **Self-review the work.** Name what's broken, thin, or skipped — and the trade-off that put it there. A calibrated gap-list with reasoning earns more credit with us than polished completeness.
- **The chat is read alongside the code.** Submit your raw AI conversations with the work — the code tells us what shipped; the chat tells us how it got there, and who was steering. Your trail of commits, conversations, and revisions tells us more about your approach than a single tidy drop.
- **You are the designer of this system.** You should be able to walk through any part of it — what it does, why it's there, where it could break.
- **The code matters as much as what it produces.** Good code is its own bar.

---

## Time

24 to 48 hours. How you allocate it is up to you.

---

## AI Tools

Use them. We want to see how you work with AI, not without it.

**Non-Negotiable Requirement:** Submit raw `.jsonl` session logs covering every phase of your work — problem framing, domain research, planning, coding, documentation, testing, and QA. Use whichever coding agents you like (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, etc.), and feel free to switch or combine them — but every phase must be done inside a coding agent that produces `.jsonl`, and the logs for all of them must be in the submission. We need the complete trail, not just the coding portion.

Curated Markdown summaries, `.json` array dumps, or screenshots DO NOT substitute — only raw `.jsonl` lets us separate your contribution from the agent's. DO NOT use a tool that doesn't give you `.jsonl` files for any phase.

> **Mandatory — do not skip.** Not sure where your logs are? Just ask your agent — "where are my session logs / JSONL files?" is a valid prompt and it should be able to locate them for you. Submissions without complete session logs (covering all phases) will BE REJECTED by default.

We'll look at:
- How you prompt
- Whether you iterate or just accept
- What you caught that AI got wrong
- Whether you understand the code you submitted

---

## What to Submit

| Deliverable | What We Want to See |
|-------------|---------------------|
| **`.git` folder** | Your commit history — we review how you approached the problem. |
| **JSONL session logs** | Raw session logs covering every phase (framing, research, coding, documentation, testing, QA). One or many agents — but the logs must cover all phases. Shows how you think, prompt, and iterate. |
| **README** | Setup instructions. We will clone your submission and run it — if it doesn't run, the review stops. |
| **Domain model doc** | Your entities, relationships, state machines. Why this decomposition? |
| **Decisions doc** | What you built, what you didn't, what assumptions you made. |
| **Self-review** | Honest assessment. What's good? What's rough? What would you change with more time? |
| **Working system + tests** | Does what it claims. Tests encode domain rules, not just assert HTTP status codes. |

> **Submissions missing any required deliverable will be rejected.** Don't let a missing doc cost you the opportunity.

**Submission format:** Zip/tarball with `.git` folder included. Commit history matters.

---

## What We Don't Prescribe

- Technology stack
- How to structure your code
- Which edge cases to handle
- How sophisticated to make it
- What the interface looks like

**Make decisions. Justify them.**

---

## What Makes a Good Submission

**Coherent scope.** A system that does 3 things well beats one that does 10 things poorly.

**Clear domain model.** We should understand your abstractions and why you chose them.

**Tests written before code.** Your git history should show tests appearing before or alongside implementation — not added at the end. Tests that specify behavior ("when a claim is submitted with a duplicate line item, it should...") are far more valuable than tests that just check return types.

**Honest self-assessment.** "This is rough because..." shows more maturity than "everything is perfect."

**Effective AI use.** Using AI to explore options and iterate is good. Accepting walls of code without review is a red flag.

---

## What Gets Rejected

**Submissions missing any required deliverable will be rejected.** Beyond that, these are the patterns that lead to a rejection:

- No AI session logs, or partial logs covering only some phases (we can't evaluate your process)
- No `.git` folder or a single-commit dump (we can't see how you worked)
- No domain model documentation, or a domain model that doesn't go beyond what AI generated without your input
- Can't explain your own code — if you can't walk through it, it's not yours
- No tests, or tests clearly written after the fact (git history doesn't lie)
- Scattered half-features with no coherence
- Self-review that doesn't match reality

---

## Next Round

If you proceed, we'll extend your system together in a 75-minute pairing session. Write code you can navigate and modify under pressure.

---

## Questions

Logistics → reach out to Sumanth Raj Urs (sumanth@realfast.ai).

Requirements → part of the assignment. Make assumptions, document them.

lastPrompt: ALSO run a analysis on the  problem statement are the questions that and problems shown are fulfilled  and shown in the flowchart diagram?

content.text:
The problem statement is rich — it has explicit "interesting problems" (5 questions), a working-system checklist, in-scope items, and evaluation signals. Notably it lists the lifecycle as `submitted → under review → approved/denied → **paid**`, while your design deliberately dropped PAID (decision #14) — exactly the kind of divergence this analysis must surface.

This is a traceability/coverage question, so I'll run a second workflow (concurrent with the DBA one): **Extract** atomic requirements → **Trace** each against the flowchart → **adversarially Verify** every "shown" claim against the actual file → **Synthesize** a coverage report answering your question directly.

content.text:
Both workflows are now running concurrently in the background:

| Workflow | Run ID | Produces |
|---|---|---|
| 🗄️ DBA physical ERD | `wf_fa611403-41e` | `docs/erd-physical.md` |
| 📋 Problem-statement coverage | `wf_40e9ad29-fd8` | `docs/problem-statement-coverage.md` |

**What the coverage analysis checks** — for every atomic requirement in the problem statement, it assigns a flowchart verdict (`SHOWN` / `PARTIAL` / `NOT_SHOWN` / `N/A`) backed by an exact node or matrix-row citation, then a separate adversarial verifier re-reads the actual file to confirm or refute each claim (default to the weaker verdict when evidence is missing). It explicitly separates three things:

- ✅ **Fulfilled & shown** in the flowchart (e.g. mixed-outcome aggregation, limit exhaustion, dispute/retroactive-change flow, denial reason codes).
- ⚠️ **Gaps** — required but not in the flowchart.
- 🔀 **Deliberate divergences** — e.g. the statement's `…→ paid` state vs. your decision #14 to drop PAID (a conscious trade-off, not a bug).
- 📂 **N/A-for-flowchart** items (interface, coverage-rule representation) — with a pointer to where they *are* covered.

I'll report back with findings from each as they finish, and validate the artifacts myself before walking you through them. Watch live with `/workflows`.

N/A items (rule representation, entity decomposition, member-policy binding, interface choice, PHI/security, schema/API/UI, and 8 process deliverables, plus the negative out-of-scope requirement) correctly live in domain-model.md, erd.md, decisions.md, PRD.md, ai-artifacts/, README, and git history. Three deliberate divergences are called out for reviewers as conscious trade-offs, not bugs: dropped PAID state, no cross-claim cascade, and immutable original adjudications."}</result>

content.text: The coverage analysis finished (43 agents, ~40 requirements traced + adversarially verified). Let me verify the written report before relaying.

content.content:
1	# Problem-Statement Coverage Analysis — Scenario Flowchart
2	
3	> Scope: judges whether the problem-statement questions/problems are FULFILLED and visually SHOWN in `docs/scenarios-flowchart.md` (5 Mermaid flowcharts + 45-row scenario coverage matrix); cross-references the design docs where a concern legitimately lives off-diagram.
4	
5	## Headline verdict
6	
7	**Yes — every problem-statement concern that *belongs* in a scenario flowchart is shown, and the rest is correctly delegated to the design docs.** Of 40 traced requirements: **19 are SHOWN** (fully drawn as nodes/branches), **5 are PARTIAL** (the decision-classification half is drawn; the human-readable/text-generation half is doc-side), and **16 are N/A for a flowchart** (structural, security, interface, or process deliverables that a behavioral flowchart cannot and should not depict).
8	
9	**Zero true gaps.** No requirement that *should* appear in a flowchart is missing. The 5 PARTIALs all share one root cause (explanation **reason codes** are shown; explanation **text/EOB builder** is doc-side) and one deliberate divergence (the brief's literal `PAID` lifecycle state is intentionally not modeled — decision #14). The trace and the adversarial verifier **agreed on all 40 rows**; there are no disagreements to flag.
10	
11	## Traceability matrix
12	
13	| ID | Requirement | Category | Flowchart verdict | Evidence (diagram / node) | Also covered in |
14	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
15	| Q1 | Model coverage rules (service type X covered up to $Y/yr) | interesting-problem | N/A_FLOWCHART | FC2 only *consults* rule fields: Step 2 "Rule exists for serviceCode?", Step 3 "Covered && !excluded?", Step 5 limit gates, Step 7 "Cost-share switch on costShare.type" — never depicts rule structure/storage | domain-model.md:80-108; erd.md:37-49,140-176; PRD.md:87-108; decisions.md #3,#6,#7 |
16	| Q2 | Track what's already used against limits | interesting-problem | SHOWN | FC2 Step 5 reads prior usage (VISCHECK `used_count<count`, DOLCHECK `used_cents<amountCents`); Step 9 writeback `deltas.limit_inc`; Step 7b straddle; matrix CROSS-LINE rows + Axiom 3 (accrual) | adjudication-plan.md:122-137; domain-model.md:25; erd.md ACCUMULATOR |
17	| Q3 | Mixed-outcome aggregation (3 covered/1 denied/1 review) | interesting-problem | SHOWN | FC3 CHECK1 (any NEEDS_REVIEW/disputed → UNDER_REVIEW) precedes CHECK2/CHECK3; PARTIAL node catches approved+denied mix; matrix AGGREGATION:* rows | domain-model.md:168-175; adjudication-plan.md:86-97 |
18	| Q4 | Explain WHY something was denied | interesting-problem | PARTIAL | FC2 emits a reason code at every terminal node; matrix has Reason Code(s) column. Missing: human-readable EOB text builder / `/explanation` endpoint (no node) | adjudication-plan.md:34-42,99-109; domain-model.md:228-236; PRD.md:42,64 |
19	| Q5 | State machine of claim vs. line item (two lifecycles) | interesting-problem | SHOWN | FC5 distinguishes `CLAIM:` and `LINE_ITEM:` transition chains explicitly; FC3 derives claim status; PARTIALLY_APPROVED noted claim-level-only | domain-model.md:140-166; erd.md:182-187 |
20	| WS1 | Accept claim submissions with line items | working-system-capability | SHOWN | FC1 START → VALIDATE ("≥1 line item") → PERSIST (Claim=SUBMITTED, LineItems=PENDING) → 200 | PRD.md:37,174-180; domain-model.md:71-78 |
21	| WS2 | Apply coverage rules → payable amounts | working-system-capability | SHOWN | FC2 Steps 6-9: allowed=billed, cost-share switch (FC/COPAY/COINS), APPROVED node `payable_cents`/`member_resp_cents` | adjudication-plan.md:47-84; PRD.md:39 |
22	| WS3 | Move claims through lifecycle states | working-system-capability | SHOWN | FC5 null→SUBMITTED→terminal + dispute reopen; FC3 aggregation to terminal | domain-model.md:140-166; PRD.md:40 |
23	| WS4 | Produce explanations for every decision | working-system-capability | PARTIAL | Reason code at every FC2 terminal node + matrix column. Missing: explanation-text generation | adjudication-plan.md:34-42,99-109; PRD.md:42,64,83 |
24	| WS5 | Demonstration interface (REST/UI/CLI — pick one) | working-system-capability | N/A_FLOWCHART | Interface choice is system-design, not a flow. FC1/FC4 only annotate HTTP 200/400/404/409/4xx as a side effect | decisions.md #5 (REST/fastify); PRD.md:58-69 |
25	| SCOPE1 | Submitting a claim with line items | in-scope-item | SHOWN | FC1 end-to-end: VALIDATE → RESOLVE → FP → PERSIST | PRD.md:174-185; domain-model.md:71-78 |

# Problem-Statement Coverage Analysis — Scenario Flowchart

> Scope: judges whether the problem-statement questions/problems are FULFILLED and visually SHOWN in `docs/scenarios-flowchart.md` (5 Mermaid flowcharts + 45-row scenario coverage matrix); cross-references the design docs where a concern legitimately lives off-diagram.

## Headline verdict

**Yes — every problem-statement concern that *belongs* in a scenario flowchart is shown, and the rest is correctly delegated to the design docs.** Of 40 traced requirements: **19 are SHOWN** (fully drawn as nodes/branches), **5 are PARTIAL** (the decision-classification half is drawn; the human-readable/text-generation half is doc-side), and **16 are N/A for a flowchart** (structural, security, interface, or process deliverables that a behavioral flowchart cannot and should not depict).

**Zero true gaps.** No requirement that *should* appear in a flowchart is missing. The 5 PARTIALs all share one root cause (explanation **reason codes** are shown; explanation **text/EOB builder** is doc-side) and one deliberate divergence (the brief's literal `PAID` lifecycle state is intentionally not modeled — decision #14). The trace and the adversarial verifier **agreed on all 40 rows**; there are no disagreements to flag.

## Traceability matrix

## Gaps & partials (concerns that *could* live in a flowchart but don't, or only partly do)

The 5 PARTIALs are the only items where flowchart-eligible content is incomplete. There are **no NOT_SHOWN gaps**.

| ID(s) | Severity | What's missing on-diagram | Concrete recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q4 / WS4 / SCOPE4 / SIGNAL7 (same root cause) | **Low** | Reason **codes** are emitted at every FC2 terminal node and in the matrix, but the **explanation-text / EOB builder** (turning codes into a member-facing sentence) and the `/explanation` endpoint appear in no node. This is correctly a system/data concern living in adjudication-plan.md + PRD.md. | Optional, low-value: add a single trailing node to **Flowchart 2** after the terminal states — e.g. `EXPLAIN["Build explanation: map reason codes → EOB text"]` — purely to make the doc-side text-generation step visible as a downstream stage. Not required; the reason classifier (the flowchart-appropriate half) is already fully shown. |
| FLOW5 | **Low** (and partly a deliberate divergence — see below) | Within this file there is **no in-diagram annotation** that PAID is intentionally deferred; a reviewer reading only the flowchart sees PAID simply omitted. | Optional: add a footnote near **Flowchart 5** (e.g. "Terminal states are final for v1; PAID/payment lifecycle deferred — see decision #14") so the omission reads as deliberate without opening decisions.md. The behavioral lifecycle itself needs no new node. |

## Deliberate divergences from the problem statement (conscious trade-offs, NOT bugs)

- **`PAID` lifecycle state dropped (decision #14).** The brief literally names the arc `submitted → under_review → approved/denied → PAID`. The design **intentionally omits PAID** as a v2 deferral: payment/settlement is out of scope for v1, so the flowcharts correctly model only `SUBMITTED → {APPROVED | PARTIALLY_APPROVED | DENIED | UNDER_REVIEW}` as terminal. This is a documented trade-off (decisions.md #14; domain-model.md:136,161; erd.md:243; adjudication-plan.md:96,317; PRD.md:40,218), not an oversight. **Flag for reviewers:** the flowchart's terminal set deliberately differs from the brief's verbatim arc.
- **No cross-claim cascade on dispute (Axiom #5 / FC4 edge note).** Retroactive re-adjudication nets out the disputed line's own accumulator deltas but does **not** re-run intervening sibling lines across other claims. Explicitly labelled "documented v1 limitation" inside the flowchart file — a conscious scoping decision feeding self-review.
- **Original adjudication is immutable (Axiom #4 / FC4 PERSIST).** Re-adjudication appends a new Adjudication row rather than mutating the original — a deliberate audit-integrity choice, surfaced as a divergence from a naive "update in place" model.

| **Entity decomposition** (Member/Policy/CoverageRule/Claim/LineItem/Adjudication/Accumulator/Dispute/StatusTransition) | SIGNAL1, PROC4 | domain-model.md:15-37; erd.md:7-122 |
| **Member→Policy→Rule binding** (setup relationship) | FLOW1 | domain-model.md:15-37,80-108; erd.md:9-49 |
| **Interface / transport choice** (REST vs UI vs CLI) | WS5 | decisions.md #5; PRD.md:58-69 (HTTP codes in FC1/FC4 lightly imply REST) |
| **PHI sensitivity / security** (minimization, encryption-at-rest, engine isolation) | FLOW3 | domain-model.md:44-46,68; decisions.md #9; adjudication-plan.md:139-144; PRD.md:144-145,214 |
| **DB schema / API design / UI** (open design choices) | NSPEC1 | erd.md; PRD.md:58-69,87-108; decisions.md |
| **Process deliverables** (domain research, test-first discipline, JSONL logs, decisions doc, self-review, README, git history) | PROC1–PROC8 | ai-artifacts/; decisions.md; self-review.md; README.md; .git/ history; (matrix supports PROC2 as a test blueprint) |
| **Negative requirement** (out-of-scope features must be ABSENT) | SCOPEOUT1 | PRD.md:45-56,67 — verified absent across all 5 diagrams |

lastPrompt: ALSO run a analysis on the  problem statement are the questions that and problems shown are fulfilled  and shown in the flowchart diagram?

content.text:
📋 Coverage analysis complete — report written to [docs/problem-statement-coverage.md](docs/problem-statement-coverage.md). It traced **40 atomic requirements** from the problem statement; each got a flowchart verdict, then a *separate adversarial verifier* re-read the file to confirm or refute it (they agreed on all 40 rows, zero disagreements).

## ✅ Direct answer: Yes — with two honest caveats

> **Every problem-statement concern that belongs in a flowchart is shown. Zero true gaps** (nothing flowchart-eligible is missing). The remainder is correctly delegated to the design docs.

| Verdict | Count | Meaning |
|---|---:|---|
| ✅ **SHOWN** | 19 | Fully drawn as nodes/branches |
| ⚠️ **PARTIAL** | 5 | Half drawn; other half is doc-side/deferred |
| 📂 **N/A for flowchart** | 16 | Structural/security/interface/process — belongs elsewhere |
| ❌ **NOT_SHOWN** | 0 | No missing flowchart-eligible requirement |

## 🎯 The 5 "interesting problems" (the brief's core questions)

| # | Question | In flowchart? | Where |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | Model coverage rules ($Y/yr) | 📂 N/A | FC2 *applies* rules; structure lives in `domain-model.md` / `erd.md` |
| Q2 | Track usage against limits | ✅ SHOWN | FC2 Step 5 (`used_count<count`, `used_cents<amountCents`) + Step 9 writeback |
| Q3 | 5-line mixed outcome (3✓/1✗/1 review) | ✅ SHOWN | FC3 aggregation — NEEDS_REVIEW precedence → PARTIALLY_APPROVED |
| Q4 | Explain WHY denied | ⚠️ PARTIAL | Reason **code** at every terminal node; EOB **text builder** is doc-side |
| Q5 | Claim vs line-item state machine | ✅ SHOWN | FC5 distinguishes `CLAIM:` and `LINE_ITEM:` chains |

**Edge-case signals** the brief flagged are all shown: partial approvals (FC2 straddle + FC3), limit exhaustion (FC2 `VISLIM`/`DOLLIM`), and retroactive changes (FC4 dispute re-adjudication against *current* rules).

## ⚠️ The only soft spot — all 5 PARTIALs share one root cause

Explanation **reason codes** are drawn at every denial node and in the matrix, but the **human-readable EOB text builder** (`/explanation`) appears in no node — it lives in `adjudication-plan.md` + `PRD.md`. **Severity: Low.** Optional fix: append one `EXPLAIN` node after FC2's terminals to make that downstream stage visible.

## 🔀 Deliberate divergences — flag these to reviewers (not bugs)

1. **`PAID` state dropped (decision #14).** The brief literally says `…→ approved/denied → PAID`; your flowcharts end at `{APPROVED | PARTIALLY_APPROVED | DENIED}`. Conscious v2 deferral. ⤷ *Recommend a one-line footnote near FC5* so a reviewer reading only the flowchart sees the omission is intentional.
2. **No cross-claim cascade** on dispute re-adjudication (labelled a documented v1 limitation inside FC4).
3. **Immutable original adjudication** — disputes append a new row vs. update-in-place (audit-integrity choice).

## 📂 Correctly NOT in the flowchart (16 items)
Entity decomposition, coverage-rule *representation*, member→policy binding, interface choice (REST), PHI/security, DB schema/API/UI, and 8 process deliverables (JSONL logs, tests, README, git history) — all verified to live in the right docs. The out-of-scope features (auth, dashboards, admin) were confirmed **absent** from all 5 diagrams.

---

🗄️ The **DBA physical-ERD workflow** (`wf_fa611403-41e`) is still running — I'll report on it when it lands.

### Follow-up questions
1. Want me to apply the two low-severity flowchart tweaks — the `EXPLAIN` node after FC2 and the PAID-deferral footnote near FC5 — so the flowchart is self-explanatory without opening `decisions.md`?
2. Should I run the same trace against **`PRD.md` acceptance criteria** (not just the brief) to catch any requirement that exists in your PRD but isn't drawn anywhere?
3. Do you want a one-page **reviewer-facing "divergences & limitations" summary** (PAID, no cross-claim cascade, immutable adjudication, EOB-text-deferred) to drop into `docs/self-review.md`, since the brief rewards a calibrated gap-list?

But the synthesis made one consequential call I need to scrutinize against the source docs before I endorse it: it put a **catalog CHECK on `line_items.service_code`**, which would reject an unlisted code at **intake** — yet the domain model and your own scenario flowchart say an unlisted code is *accepted at intake and denied `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication*. Let me read the exact wording in the PRD and decision #10 to adjudicate fairly.

### Service-code catalog (closed, 12)

`PREVENTIVE` · `PCP_VISIT` · `SPECIALIST_VISIT` · `URGENT_CARE` · `EMERGENCY_ROOM` · `LAB` ·
`MRI` · `OUTPATIENT_SURGERY` · `INPATIENT_HOSPITAL` · `PHYSICAL_THERAPY` · `CHIROPRACTIC` ·
`ADULT_DENTAL`. An unlisted `service_code` is *accepted* at intake and *denied* `NO_COVERAGE`
at adjudication — never an intake reject.

### Intake pipeline (C2) — N1–N5

1. Receive `POST /claims`.
2. **Structural validation**: required fields, ≥1 line, `billed_cents` a positive integer, `service_date` a real non-future date.
3. **Member resolution**: `member_id` must resolve to a known Member, else reject.
4. **Compute** the per-line fingerprint.
5. **Persist** Claim + LineItems (claim `SUBMITTED`, lines `PENDING`).

**Reject model:** a structural/identity failure → HTTP `4xx` with `{ errors: [{ field, code,
message }] }` and **nothing persisted** (there is no `REJECTED` state — a reject never enters
the system). Member *existence* is an intake reject; policy *active-on-date* is an
adjudication deny (`POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE`).

content.text:
The PRD is explicit and decisive:

> **PRD.md:171** — *"An unlisted `service_code` is accepted at intake and denied `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication — **never an intake reject**."*

A `CHECK` on `line_items.service_code` makes the intake `INSERT` fail for an unlisted code — i.e. exactly the "intake reject" the PRD forbids. Three independent sources (PRD:171, domain-model:66, and your own flowchart matrix row "Service code not in catalog → NO_COVERAGE at adjudication, HTTP 200") all contradict the DBA's synthesis call. This isn't a judgment fork — it's a spec violation the adversarial review resolved the wrong way. I'll fix it: keep the catalog `CHECK` on `coverage_rules` (config data), but make `line_items.service_code` and `disputes.corrected_service_code` free text.

Let me read the exact DDL/rationale blocks to edit precisely.

) STRICT;

-- =====================================================================
-- 5) line_items — unit of adjudication.
-- =====================================================================
CREATE TABLE line_items (
  id                 TEXT    NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
  claim_id           TEXT    NOT NULL,
  service_code       TEXT    NOT NULL,               -- closed catalog; rule-miss -> NO_COVERAGE at adj
  billed_cents       INTEGER NOT NULL,
  units              INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  prior_auth_present INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  status             TEXT    NOT NULL DEFAULT 'PENDING',
  fingerprint        TEXT    NOT NULL,
  created_at         TEXT    NOT NULL DEFAULT (strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%fZ','now')),
  updated_at         TEXT    NOT NULL DEFAULT (strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%fZ','now')),
  CONSTRAINT fk_line_items_claim
    FOREIGN KEY (claim_id) REFERENCES claims(id) ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE,
  CONSTRAINT ck_li_service_code CHECK (service_code IN (
    'PREVENTIVE','PCP_VISIT','SPECIALIST_VISIT','URGENT_CARE','EMERGENCY_ROOM','LAB',
    'MRI','OUTPATIENT_SURGERY','INPATIENT_HOSPITAL','PHYSICAL_THERAPY','CHIROPRACTIC','ADULT_DENTAL')),
  CONSTRAINT ck_li_billed_pos CHECK (billed_cents > 0),
  CONSTRAINT ck_li_units_pos  CHECK (units > 0),
  CONSTRAINT ck_li_pa_bool    CHECK (prior_auth_present IN (0,1)),
  CONSTRAINT ck_li_status     CHECK (status IN ('PENDING','APPROVED','DENIED','NEEDS_REVIEW'))
) STRICT;

-- =====================================================================
-- 6) adjudications — APPEND-ONLY immutable decisions. current = MAX(seq).
--    Carries billed_cents snapshot + plan_year for self-contained audit.

    FOREIGN KEY (original_adjudication_id, line_item_id)

    FOREIGN KEY (resolved_adjudication_id, line_item_id)
    REFERENCES adjudications(id, line_item_id) ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE,
  CONSTRAINT ck_disputes_state   CHECK (state IN ('OPEN','RESOLVED')),
  CONSTRAINT ck_disputes_outcome CHECK (outcome IS NULL OR outcome IN ('UPHELD','OVERTURNED','PARTIALLY_OVERTURNED','MODIFIED')),
  CONSTRAINT ck_disputes_corr_pa   CHECK (corrected_prior_auth_present IS NULL OR corrected_prior_auth_present IN (0,1)),
  CONSTRAINT ck_disputes_corr_bill CHECK (corrected_billed_cents IS NULL OR corrected_billed_cents > 0),

  CONSTRAINT ck_disputes_corr_sc   CHECK (corrected_service_code IS NULL OR corrected_service_code IN (
    'PREVENTIVE','PCP_VISIT','SPECIALIST_VISIT','URGENT_CARE','EMERGENCY_ROOM','LAB',
    'MRI','OUTPATIENT_SURGERY','INPATIENT_HOSPITAL','PHYSICAL_THERAPY','CHIROPRACTIC','ADULT_DENTAL')),
  -- state coherence: OPEN has no resolution; RESOLVED is fully populated:
  CONSTRAINT ck_disputes_state_shape CHECK (
       (state = 'OPEN'     AND resolved_adjudication_id IS NULL     AND outcome IS NULL     AND resolved_at IS NULL)
    OR (state = 'RESOLVED' AND resolved_adjudication_id IS NOT NULL  AND outcome IS NOT NULL  AND resolved_at IS NOT NULL)

746	The original design modeled `service_code` as **free text** with explicit "NO catalog CHECK." Three locked docs disagree: domain-model.md L54/L63–66, PRD.md L167–172, and **decision #10** all define a **closed 12-entry catalog**. **The locked decision wins.** Both `line_items.service_code` and `coverage_rules.service_code` (and `disputes.corrected_service_code` when set) carry a CHECK constraining values to `PREVENTIVE, PCP_VISIT, SPECIALIST_VISIT, URGENT_CARE, EMERGENCY_ROOM, LAB, MRI, OUTPATIENT_SURGERY, INPATIENT_HOSPITAL, PHYSICAL_THERAPY, CHIROPRACTIC, ADULT_DENTAL`. The "unlisted → NO_COVERAGE" rule is **not** an arbitrary string at intake — it is a catalog code with **no matching `coverage_rules` row on this policy** (a rule-lookup miss), denied `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication. This preserves the decision-vs-error boundary (a catalog code is accepted at intake; the coverage decision happens at adjudication) while honoring the closed catalog. The original free-text justification conflated the *service-code catalog* (closed enum) with *coverage existence* (a per-policy rule lookup) — they are different things.

input.new_string:
### service_code: catalog CHECK on coverage_rules, FREE TEXT on line_items (review finding #catalog — CORRECTED)
A reviewer pushed to constrain every `service_code` to the closed 12-entry catalog. That is correct for **`coverage_rules.service_code`** (seeded config — a rule should only exist for a known service), and the catalog CHECK stays there. It is **wrong for `line_items.service_code`** (and `disputes.corrected_service_code`): the locked spec is explicit that an unlisted code is *accepted* at intake and *denied* `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication — **"never an intake reject"** (PRD.md:171; domain-model.md:66; the scenario-flowchart matrix row *"Service code not in catalog → `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication, HTTP 200"*). A DB `CHECK` on `line_items.service_code` would fail the intake `INSERT` for an unlisted code, turning an adjudication **decision** (`NO_COVERAGE`, HTTP 200, with an explanation) into a structural **error** (HTTP 4xx) — violating the decision-vs-error boundary and the "explain WHY denied" capability the brief rewards. So `line_items.service_code` is **free text (NOT NULL, no catalog CHECK)**; coverage is a `(policy_id, service_code)` lookup against `coverage_rules`, and `NO_COVERAGE` covers *both* an unlisted code *and* a catalog code with no matching rule on the policy. Decision #10's "closed catalog" governs what gets a *coverage rule*, not what intake *accepts*.

Decision #10's "closed catalog" governs what gets a *coverage rule*, not what intake *accepts*.

lines: `accumulators.unit` (`CENTS`|`COUNT`) discriminant added. `ck_acc_unit_dimension`: `DEDUCTIBLE`/`OOP` are always `CENTS` with `used_count = 0`; a `LIMIT:%` row picks exactly one column by `unit` (CENTS ⇒ `used_count=0`, COUNT ⇒ `used_cents=0`). This closes the hole where a visit-limit row could store dollars in `used_cents` and be silently misread as 0 visits used. Plan-year reset = **new rows** per `plan_year` (the `(member_id, plan_year, dimension)` uniqueness makes each year fresh), never in-place — prior-year totals are preserved for audit. `accumulators` is the **one** UPDATE-in-place table; immutable history lives in the adjudication deltas.
-### service_code: closed 12-entry catalog (review finding #catalog, major — CONFLICT RESOLVED)

-The original design modeled `service_code` as **free text** with explicit "NO catalog CHECK." Three locked docs disagree: domain-model.md L54/L63–66, PRD.md L167–172, and **decision #10** all define a **closed 12-entry catalog**. **The locked decision wins.** Both `line_items.service_code` and `coverage_rules.service_code` (and `disputes.corrected_service_code` when set) carry a CHECK constraining values to `PREVENTIVE, PCP_VISIT, SPECIALIST_VISIT, URGENT_CARE, EMERGENCY_ROOM, LAB, MRI, OUTPATIENT_SURGERY, INPATIENT_HOSPITAL, PHYSICAL_THERAPY, CHIROPRACTIC, ADULT_DENTAL`. The "unlisted → NO_COVERAGE" rule is **not** an arbitrary string at intake — it is a catalog code with **no matching `coverage_rules` row on this policy** (a rule-lookup miss), denied `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication. This preserves the decision-vs-error boundary (a catalog code is accepted at intake; the coverage decision happens at adjudication) while honoring the closed catalog. The original free-text justification conflated the *service-code catalog* (closed enum) with *coverage existence* (a per-policy rule lookup) — they are different things.
+### service_code: catalog CHECK on coverage_rules, FREE TEXT on line_items (review finding #catalog — CORRECTED)

+A reviewer pushed to constrain every `service_code` to the closed 12-entry catalog. That is correct for **`coverage_rules.service_code`** (seeded config — a rule should only exist for a known service), and the catalog CHECK stays there. It is **wrong for `line_items.service_code`** (and `disputes.corrected_service_code`): the locked spec is explicit that an unlisted code is *accepted* at intake and *denied* `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication — **"never an intake reject"** (PRD.md:171; domain-model.md:66; the scenario-flowchart matrix row *"Service code not in catalog → `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication, HTTP 200"*). A DB `CHECK` on `line_items.service_code` would fail the intake `INSERT` for an unlisted code, turning an adjudication **decision** (`NO_COVERAGE`, HTTP 200, with an explanation) into a structural **error** (HTTP 4xx) — violating the decision-vs-error boundary and the "explain WHY denied" capability the brief rewards. So `line_items.service_code` is **free text (NOT NULL, no catalog CHECK)**; coverage is a `(policy_id, service_code)` lookup against `coverage_rules`, and `NO_COVERAGE` covers *both* an unlisted code *and* a catalog code with no matching rule on the policy. Decision #10's "closed catalog" governs what gets a *coverage rule*, not what intake *accepts*.

`members.name`/`dob`, `claims.provider`/`diagnosis_code` are PHI, isolated to member/claim records; the adjudication engine input is typed to exclude them (keys on `member_id` → policy + accumulators), so it **structurally** never reads them. SQLite has no native column encryption; documented approach is SQLCipher whole-file encryption-at-rest or app-level field encryption before insert.

# 🗄️ Physical ERD complete → [docs/erd-physical.md](docs/erd-physical.md)

The DBA workflow (design → 3 adversarial reviewers → synthesize → on-disk verify) produced a **836-line** physical schema for your real stack: **SQLite / better-sqlite3 / Drizzle**, all 9 tables as `STRICT` tables with full DDL, indexes, append-only triggers, a per-table attribute reference, enum value sets, and a Drizzle/zod mapping.

## 🔗 Table relationships (full attribute-level diagram + DDL are in the file)

```mermaid
erDiagram
  MEMBERS ||--|| POLICIES : "has 1 per plan_year"
  MEMBERS ||--o{ CLAIMS : "submits"
  MEMBERS ||--o{ ACCUMULATORS : "tracked by (1+/plan_year)"
  POLICIES ||--o{ COVERAGE_RULES : "defines"
  POLICIES ||--o{ CLAIMS : "adjudicated under"
  CLAIMS ||--o{ LINE_ITEMS : "contains 1+"
  LINE_ITEMS ||--o{ ADJUDICATIONS : "decided by (append-only; MAX seq = current)"
  LINE_ITEMS ||--o{ DISPUTES : "challenged by (terminal only)"
  ADJUDICATIONS ||--o{ DISPUTES : "original + resolved (composite FK)"
  CLAIMS ||--o{ STATUS_TRANSITIONS : "logged via claim_id"
  LINE_ITEMS ||--o{ STATUS_TRANSITIONS : "logged via line_item_id"
```

## 📋 The 9 tables — what each carries

| Table | Key columns | Notable constraints |
|---|---|---|
| **members** | `id` PK, `name`/`dob` (PHI) | PHI flagged for app-level encryption (SQLCipher) |
| **policies** | `id` PK, `member_id` FK, `plan_year`, `deductible_cents`, `oop_max_cents` | `UNIQUE(member_id, plan_year)`; `oop_max ≥ deductible` |
| **coverage_rules** | `id` PK, `policy_id` FK, `service_code`, `cost_share_type`+`copay_cents`/`coinsurance_rate`, `limit_unit`+`limit_amount_cents`/`limit_count` | `UNIQUE(policy_id, service_code)`; **discriminated-union CHECKs** (copay_cents non-null iff copay, etc.); **catalog CHECK here** |
| **claims** | `id` PK, `member_id`+`policy_id` FK, `service_date`, `provider`/`diagnosis_code` (PHI), `status` | status enum; policy_id recorded at intake for audit |
| **line_items** | `id` PK, `claim_id` FK, `service_code` *(free text)*, `billed_cents`, `units`, `prior_auth_present`, `fingerprint` | `billed>0`, `units>0`, bool CHECKs; **no catalog CHECK** (fixed) |
| **adjudications** | `id` PK, `line_item_id` FK, `seq`, `payable_cents`, `member_responsibility_cents`, `reasons_json`, `delta_*` | **append-only** (triggers RAISE on UPDATE/DELETE); `payable+member==billed`; current = `MAX(seq)` |
| **accumulators** | `id` PK, `member_id` FK, `plan_year`, `dimension`, `unit`, `used_cents`/`used_count` | `UNIQUE(member, plan_year, dimension)`; unit ties value column to rule unit |
| **disputes** | `id` PK, `line_item_id` FK, `original`+`resolved_adjudication_id` (composite FK), `corrected_*`, `outcome`, `state` | partial `UNIQUE … WHERE state=OPEN`; resolved nullable while OPEN |
| **status_transitions** | `id` PK, `entity_type`, `claim_id`+`line_item_id` FK, `from`/`to_status`, `actor`, `reason`, `seq` | **append-only**; polymorphic exactly-one; `UNIQUE(entity, seq)` |

## 🛠️ The one correctness bug I caught & fixed

The synthesis "resolved" a reviewer conflict the wrong way — it constrained `line_items.service_code` to the 12-code catalog, citing decision #10. **That violates the spec:**

> **PRD.md:171** — *"An unlisted `service_code` is accepted at intake and denied `NO_COVERAGE` at adjudication — never an intake reject."*

A DB `CHECK` turns the intake `INSERT` into a 4xx error for an unlisted code, converting an adjudication **decision** (`NO_COVERAGE`, HTTP 200, *with an explanation*) into a structural **error** — and your flowchart matrix already encodes the correct behavior. I removed the CHECK from `line_items` + `disputes.corrected_service_code` (kept it on `coverage_rules`) and rewrote the rationale section.

> 📌 **Why it slipped:** the integrity reviewer (rightly) flagged free-text as a data smell; the domain reviewer's "accepted at intake" point lost the tie-break. The adversarial review *surfaced* the tension — the synthesis just picked wrong.

## 🧠 Schema design highlights (SQLite-accurate)
- **Enums** → `TEXT + CHECK(... IN ...)`; **booleans** → `INTEGER CHECK IN (0,1)`; **money** → `INTEGER` cents; **dates** → `TEXT` ISO-8601 with `GLOB` guards.
- **Discriminated unions** unrepresentable-when-wrong (a copay with a coinsurance rate can't be stored).
- **Append-only** `adjudications`/`status_transitions` enforced by `BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE` triggers, not just app discipline.
- **Polymorphic `status_transitions`** resolved as two nullable FKs (`claim_id`, `line_item_id`) + exactly-one CHECK — so referential integrity holds (better than a bare polymorphic id).
- **Dispute integrity**: composite FK `(adjudication_id, line_item_id)` guarantees a dispute can only reference adjudications of *its own* line.
